claim: The Voynich text uses a homophonic substitution cipher where word-final glyphs {y, n, l, r, o} function as morphological suffixes encoding inflectional endings of a single underlying Latin text, causing artificial vocabulary inflation and explaining the 70.1% hapax ratio.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph set {y,n,l,r,o} mirrors common Latin inflectional endings (-i, -um, -us, -is, -a)
Stripping word-final glyphs from the hapax set would reduce unique vocabulary toward a plausible Latin core
High hapax ratio (70.1%) is disproportionate to the Zipf exponent (0.8946) for natural language
Entropy 3.8627 bits is close to Latin ~4.0 bits, consistent with a near-flat homophonic expansion
evidence_against: Currier A/B split suggests two encoding schemes, complicating a single-cipher model
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is high, suggesting vocabulary diversity beyond simple inflection stripping
If suffix-stripping collapses hapaxes, the remaining stem distribution should match Latin Zipf more tightly â€” not yet confirmed
test: Strip word-final glyphs from {y,n,l,r,o} from every word token; compute new type-token ratio and Zipf exponent on the resulting stem vocabulary; compare to raw Voynich and to Latin reference corpora.
test_metric: Post-stripping hapax ratio and Zipf exponent (expected: hapax ratio drops below 40%, Zipf exponent moves toward 1.0)
test_threshold: Confirm if post-stripping hapax ratio < 45% AND Zipf exponent > 0.95; deny if hapax ratio remains above 60% after stripping

tests_run.test: strip word-final glyphs from {y,n,l,r,o} from every word token; compute new type-token ratio and zip
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

tests_run.test: strip word-final glyphs from {y,n,l,r,o} from every word token; compute new type-token ratio and zip
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If word-final glyphs are purely morphological markers added by the cipher, stripping them should dramatically reduce vocabulary size and improve Zipf fit, narrowing the solution space to suffix-augmented substitution ciphers on inflected languages.
